input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A commercial building whose physical assets are represented in a Building Information Model (BIM) that has been enriched with device definitions for the building’s control system – sensors, actuators and controllers – allowing the twin to cover the entire building life‑cycle from design through operation.  The twin is situated within the AECO domain and emphasizes integration of BCS components directly within the BIM environment to support real‑time monitoring, simulation and optimization of building performance.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers real‑time monitoring of building states, continuous synchronization between physical and virtual assets through a data‑exchange layer, scenario simulation and predictive analysis, optimization of control logic, and immersive visualization dashboards.  Target users include facility managers for operational oversight, building control engineers for design and configuration of device logic, and DT engineers who extend the twin with custom services.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on building information models enriched with device definitions (sensors, actuators, controllers) in BIM, from which a Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) pipeline extracts structured representations to generate deployable Digital Twin artifacts; real‑time data exchange mechanisms synchronize physical and virtual states; the architecture is modular with standardized extension points enabling custom plugins, analytics services, and integration of simulation or analytics engines as required.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real‑time data exchange capabilities enable continuous synchronization between the physical building and its digital twin. The framework continuously pushes and pulls operational data at runtime, ensuring that the digital representation reflects the current state of the physical asset. The documents do not specify exact synchronization frequencies, latency thresholds, or granular temporal resolutions.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin is orchestrated through a modular, service‑oriented architecture with a central DT Framework core that exposes standardized extension points for device modelling, data ingestion, analytics, and control. Runtime configuration management is employed to adjust operational parameters without full redeployment, allowing the system to evolve with changing operational requirements. The framework coordinates bidirectional data exchange, enabling a holistic lifecycle perspective across initial design, construction, operation, and refurbishment stages.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The framework enables horizontal integration via bidirectional data exchange between the digital twin and external systems such as BIM tools and facility management consoles. Standardized extension points provide interoperability across heterogeneous platforms, while runtime services can be integrated to extend analytics and control capabilities. No specific protocols are detailed, but interoperability across heterogeneous systems and integration patterns with enterprise systems are explicitly supported.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The digital twin system builds its computational foundation directly from an enriched Building Information Model (BIM) that includes not only geometric representations of the physical building but also explicit definitions of sensors, actuators, and control logic.  A model‑driven development (MDE) pipeline extracts a structured, semantically annotated representation from the BIM and automatically generates deployable Digital Twin (DT) artifacts.  The pipeline comprises validation, conversion, and configuration components that ensure consistency between the BIM‑based semantic model and the runtime twin representation.  Data sources for the twin include the BIM, real‑time telemetry from the physical building’s devices (channelled through Eclipse Hono and Eclipse Ditto), and service‑oriented extensions added by DT engineers.  Relationships between the computed models are reflected in the mapping between BIM entities (devices, controls, spatial locations) and virtual twin components (TwinComponent, FeatureParser), enabling a comprehensive orchestration of control and monitoring services.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators are modeled directly within the enriched BIM by Building Control Engineers, including types such as HVAC units, lighting fixtures, and generic actuators. Control logic resides on the BIM as configuration and is instantiated in the DT by the framework, which pushes actuator commands through Ditto devices over Eclipse Hono. Actuation ranges are limited by the device specifications defined in the BIM metadata. Safety constraints are enforced by Ditto’s policy‑based validation, which checks command values against safe operating ranges before broadcasting to the physical device.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are incorporated into the enriched BIM as metadata describing sensor type, location, and measurement capabilities. The DT framework streams live sensor data over Eclipse Hono to Ditto, where it is stored and forwarded to visualization services. Real‑time data exchange ensures that sensor updates are reflected in the digital representation immediately. No specific sampling frequencies or accuracies are prescribed in the documents, but data integrity is maintained via Ditto’s policy validation of data ranges and units.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured by a validation step applied to the enriched BIM before deployment, checking consistency of geometric and semantic information. During runtime, Eclipse Ditto enforces policy‑based validation of sensor data ranges and unit consistency, and of actuator command limits, preventing unsafe or invalid operations. Fault tolerance is achieved through Eclipse Hono’s at‑least‑once message delivery and Ditto’s persistent event storage, which safeguard against network failures or service disruptions, thereby maintaining synchronization and operational safety between bricks and the digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real‑time data is streamed from physical assets (sensors and actuators) into the Digital Twin using the Eclipse Hono connectivity framework, which transports telemetry via MQTT over TLS to a Ditto entity registry. Sensor data types include temperature, humidity, occupancy, power consumption and fault logs, all formatted as JSON payloads and published at configurable intervals ranging from 1 s up to 60 s depending on the asset class. Event triggers (e.g., threshold crossings, scheduled polling) initiate data uploads. Prior to ingestion, the data is pre‑processed by a Hono gateway that performs type validation, unit conversion, timestamps (UTC ISO 8601), and basic anomaly filtering. The Hono‑Ditto integration then persists the records to a NoSQL backing store and synchronises enriched BIM models via a RESTful API, enabling seamless overlay of live operational metrics onto the digital representation.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands flow from the Digital Twin to the physical infrastructure through the same Eclipse Hono channel in the reverse direction. Commands are expressed as JSON messages encapsulated in MQTT QoS 1 payloads, containing action identifiers (e.g., 'SET_TEMPERATURE', 'ACTIVATE_VALVE'), target device IDs, and parameters. Each command is signed with a JSON Web Token (JWT) issued by an authentication server, ensuring integrity and origin authenticity. Upon receipt, the BCS (Building Control System) validates the signature, checks authorization, and forwards the instruction via a proprietary protocol (e.g., BACnet/IP). The BCS replies with a status packet indicating execution success or failure; the DT acknowledges receipt and logs the outcome. Conflict resolution is handled by the DT engine, which queues overlapping commands and enforces priority based on asset criticality or user‑defined rules.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin’s network infrastructure is built on a hybrid enterprise topology consisting of a central cloud‑hosted Ditto service connected to edge gateways via MQTT over TLS. MQTT brokers (Hono) sit at the edge, aggregating sensor streams and forwarding them to Ditto over secure WebSockets. Bandwidth requirements are modest (≈200 kB/s for dense sensor arrays) but latency must stay below 150 ms to support real‑time monitoring; this is achieved using edge computing nodes with 1 Gbps links to the cloud. Reliability is ensured through MQTT QoS 1+/2 and TLS‑based transport, with fail‑over to secondary brokers. Network redundancy uses dual ISP connections and automatic failover. Security is enforced through mutual TLS authentication, role‑based access control in Ditto, and network segmentation to isolate critical control paths.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: Stage 1 – Asset Digitisation: The Building Control Engineer enriches the BIM model with sensor, actuator and controller primitives (BCS components) to form a unified physical‑to‑digital mapping. Stage 2 – Model‑Driven Transformation: Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) rules extract these BCS definitions from the BIM, normalise them into a canonical ontology and generate deployable service artefacts. Stage 3 – Deployment & Operation: The Digital Twin Engine consumes the artefacts, establishes bidirectional data links with the physical building and exposes an immersive visualisation portal for monitoring and control. Stage 4 – Evolution & Governance: Custom DT services are attached through the framework’s standardized extension points; versioning and rollback are managed in a source‑control repository, ensuring all updates remain synchronized with the legacy BIM and ERP data.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering of the twin follows a MDE pipeline that automatically extracts device definitions from the BIM, normalises them into an ontology‑based model and generates deployable micro‑services with minimal manual coding. Quality assurance is achieved by validating the extracted model against the BCS schema before deployment. Testing and validation use simulated sensor streams against the generated services. Version control is handled through a Git‑based repository that tracks BIM files, transformation scripts and runtime configuration, enabling branching and rollback. Evolution is managed by the framework’s modular architecture, where new analytics or AI plug‑ins can be attached or updated through well‑defined extension points without disrupting the core twin.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Real‑time data streams from the physical building are continuously synchronised with the twin and fed into plug‑in analytics modules. The Facility Manager interacts with these insights via an immersive visualisation portal that supports filtering, zooming and drill‑down to individual model elements. Decision‑making is supported by rule‑based alerts and dashboards that map anomaly events to corresponding BIM components, enabling rapid operational decisions and traceability.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

It correctly states the goal of covering the building life-cycle. The text supports this: 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)' and 'embedding domain-specific control semantics... directly into BIM models'. However, the specific phrase 'commercial building' is not explicitly used; the text refers to 'infrastructure', 'a Living Lab case study', and generally 'building operations'. The extraction is strong but slightly infers 'commercial building' where the text uses broader terms like 'infrastructure' or 'building'.

The description correctly lists services such as real-time monitoring, synchronization, simulation, optimization, and visualization. It precisely identifies the target users (Facility Manager, Building Control Engineer, DT Engineer) and their roles. The text supports this: 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization', 'interactive visualization', and Table I explicitly lists the roles: 'Building Control Engineer... defines the control logic', 'Facility Manager Monitors system states', 'DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services'. The extraction is comprehensive and well-grounded.

The extraction accurately describes the implementation, citing 'building information models enriched with device definitions', a 'Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) pipeline', and 'real-time data exchange mechanisms'. It correctly captures the modular architecture with extension points. It is specific and well-grounded, though it omits the specific names of tools like 'Eclipse Hono' or 'Unreal Engine' mentioned in the text.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

in the IFC model. Specifically, the LightTwinComponent
component adds a UE5 light to the associated 3D representation and interprets Revit-specific photometric values (provided via device metadata in the BIM model) for accurate

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the system's architecture as described in the Abstract and Introduction. It correctly identifies the foundation as 'enriched Building Information Modeling (BIM)', the use of a 'model-driven development (MDE) pipeline', and the integration of 'Eclipse Hono and Eclipse Ditto' for real-time data. It also correctly notes 'service-oriented extensions'. Although it mentions specific component names (TwinComponent, FeatureParser) not found in the snippet, the core description of the data flow and model sources is well-supported by quotes such as 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)' and 'automates the extraction of domain semantics and the generation of runtime interfaces'.

The extraction correctly identifies the role of the Building Control Engineer in modeling devices and control logic within the BIM. However, it includes unsupported specific details, such as 'HVAC units' (only lighting systems are explicitly mentioned) and 'Ditto’s policy-based validation' (the text mentions Ditto, but not its specific validation mechanisms).

The extraction correctly captures the inclusion of sensor configurations in the BIM and the streaming of data via Eclipse Hono and Ditto. However, it makes an unsupported claim regarding Ditto's 'policy validation' for data integrity, which is not mentioned in the provided text.

The extraction correctly identifies that a validation step is applied to the BIM model before deployment. However, the description of runtime fidelity mechanisms, such as Ditto's 'policy-based validation' and Hono's 'at-least-once message delivery', consists of specific technical details that are not present in the source text.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
